project_identity_truth.title: Project identity remains the public app-layer operational presentation
project_identity_truth.description: The current repository is the newer Streamlit app layer for the Salifort project, intended to present a practical, deployment-friendly, artifact-backed operational decision-support workflow rather than the original notebook-first modeling workspace.
project_identity_truth.authority_rule: If repo identity language drifts, preserve the README plus current page framing that this repo is the public app layer and not the legacy modeling workspace.
notes: This truth protects against accidentally recasting the repo as a full modeling or orchestration mono-repo before those systems exist.
The repo name may be reassessed later, but the current role should remain explicit.

public_model_truth.title: Weighted XGBoost at threshold 0.29 remains the public operational model truth
public_model_truth.description: For the public operational story presented by this repo, the final model truth remains weighted XGBoost with selected threshold 0.29, even though local rerun comparison artifacts also record a different metric leader.
public_model_truth.authority_rule: When local rerun leaderboards differ from the published public selection, preserve the explicitly declared public reference selection unless a later governed change intentionally replaces it.
notes: The builder explicitly hard-preserves the public operational selection.
This truth should remain distinguishable from local rerun comparison results.

method_truth.title: PACE is the method spine for interpreting the project lifecycle
method_truth.description: The Salifort project should be organized and later navigated through the PACE structure: Plan, Analyze, Construct, Execute. Legacy modeling workflow context may sit behind parts of Analyze and Construct, while the current app emphasizes Execute.
method_truth.authority_rule: Use PACE as the organizing frame for future navigator routing, source grouping, and drift explanation even when the current app pages are not yet explicitly labeled by phase.
notes: PACE is a structural truth for future navigation and orchestration, not a claim that current UI already uses phase-based routing.

runtime_truth.title: App runtime is artifact-backed and offline-build oriented
runtime_truth.description: The app runtime should read generated artifacts when available, fall back when necessary, and avoid retraining models or regenerating final analytics inside Streamlit runtime.
runtime_truth.authority_rule: Preserve the separation between runtime consumption and offline generation. New features may inspect artifacts but should not silently move model fitting into Streamlit runtime.
notes: Current repo state has all required V2 artifacts present, so the observable local runtime would be full artifact-backed mode.

fallback_truth.title: Fallback heuristic is exploratory support, not final model probability
fallback_truth.description: The built-in fallback explorer logic derives a screening score and related flags for interactive review when row-level artifacts are absent. It must remain clearly separated from the final weighted XGBoost probability layer.
fallback_truth.authority_rule: If fallback mode remains in future upgrades, preserve explicit labeling that the fallback score is not the final model output.
notes: This truth is a critical safeguard against misleading users when required row-level artifacts are missing.

orchestration_truth.title: Future orchestration should remain offline-first and source-governed
orchestration_truth.description: Future navigator, agent, RAG, or Airflow layers should treat the current app and artifact bundle as governed inputs. They should explain source-of-truth and drift rather than replacing existing runtime contracts by default.
orchestration_truth.authority_rule: Any future orchestration layer should preserve offline build separation, source registries, and explicit drift tracking before adding agentic automation.
notes: This is a forward governance truth for PACE Navigator work, not a claim that orchestration is implemented today.
